one_line_takeaway: LLM cascade systems (e.g., FrugalGPT-style lightweight-then-powerful pipelines) are structurally more vulnerable than a single strong LLM: a jointly-optimized adversarial suffix that coordinates attacks on both the weak prediction model and the decision/routing module can simultaneously crash accuracy (up to -84.6%), inflate cost (up to +148.9% token cost, +108.9% latency), and boost jailbreak success (up to +81.4%), transferring even to black-box commercial API cascades and surviving randomized-smoothing and filtering defenses.
key_finding: Even a naive, unoptimized Random-Noise suffix produces measurable degradation in both accuracy and inference cost across all datasets/configurations, indicating the cascade architecture is inherently fragile even to uninformative adversaries. The proposed joint SLM+DM attack outperforms Random-Noise, SLM-only, and DM-only baselines across the board. On the two-layer white-box cascade, setting the pass-rate p=0 drives cascade accuracy down by up to 84.6%; setting p=1 inflates normalized token cost by up to 148.9% and execution time by up to 108.9%. On jailbreak evaluation (WildJailbreak), the attack raises jailbreak success rate by up to 79-81.4% relative to attacking a standalone strong LLM. Table 1 (open-source-model transferability, 2-layer cascades, cost = normalized token cost) shows, e.g., cascade(1) initial accuracy 78.0%/cost 10.37 dropping to 21.0%/9.30 under attack (cascade delta -73.1% accuracy, decision-module accuracy dropping from 86.5% to 18.0%, a -79.2% delta), and cascade(8) 85.0%/41.2 dropping to 52.5%/18.4. Table 2 (API-based commercial-model cascades, cost in USD) shows e.g. cascade(21) initial 93.5% accuracy/$0.52 cost dropping to 76.0% accuracy/$0.44 under attack, and cascade(22) 94.0%/$0.38 dropping to 74.5%/$0.21, demonstrating the attack transfers even to fully black-box, dollar-metered commercial cascade APIs. Black-box transfer attacks (optimized on one cascade, applied without re-optimization to a different cascade) remain 'highly transferable' though weaker than white-box. Under defenses (Table 3, randomized smoothing on Headline/Overruling/AGnews; Table 4, PPL and regex filtering on Headline), the attack retains substantial effectiveness -- e.g. Headline initial cascade 78.00% accuracy falls to 23.00% under Attack p=0 and only recovers to 29.00% after random smoothing, and to 42.00%/50.00% after PPL/regex filtering respectively -- indicating these lightweight defenses only partially mitigate the attack. The analytical section proves that a cascade's error probability strictly exceeds that of its strongest standalone model whenever some early stage is (weakly) worse than the final stage on its routed subset, a gap that exists even without adversarial input and that adversarial perturbation further amplifies via routing-shift and conditional-gap-amplification mechanisms.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first study of adversarial attacks against LLM cascade systems (the FrugalGPT-style architecture in which a sequence of lightweight-to-powerful models are executed in order, with per-stage decision modules that decide whether to terminate early or escalate). It formalizes the cascade as a function Phi(x; F, G) with a stopping index tau and a cost function summing per-stage model and decision-module costs, then proposes a novel 'joint-target' adversarial-suffix attack that alternately optimizes two sub-suffixes: delta(f), which attacks the prediction (weak) models to increase task loss, and delta(g), which attacks the decision modules to bias continuation/termination decisions. The two phases are coordinated via a priority-constrained candidate-selection rule (Section 4.4) so that decision-phase updates do not inadvertently repair prediction errors introduced in the prediction phase. The authors also add an 'Adaptive Pass-Rate Control' mechanism (a stochastic parameter p) that lets the attacker trade off between accuracy degradation and cost inflation. Section 5 provides an analytical proof (via a partition of the routing/decision event space) that cascades are inherently more error-prone than their strongest standalone component even in the non-adversarial setting, and that adversarial perturbations amplify this gap via 'routing shift' and 'conditional gap amplification' mechanisms.
threat_model: The adversary manipulates only the input text x within a bounded perturbation neighborhood N_eps(x), instantiated as a fixed-length suffix appended to the query (following the GCG suffix-perturbation style); no internal model parameters or routing policies are altered. Two access regimes are studied: (1) White-box: the adversary has full read access to the cascade's model architectures/parameters, decision modules, and cascade algorithm (matches open-source/research deployments), enabling gradient-based (GCG) suffix optimization directly against the target cascade. (2) Black-box: the adversary interacts only with the overall system as a black box (submitting inputs, observing outputs/latency/cost), reflecting public-facing chat/API services; attacks are optimized on one cascade configuration and transferred without re-optimization to other cascade architectures/models, including API-based commercial models. Adversarial incentives named explicitly: forcing worst-case execution paths at scale (an application-layer denial-of-service-like resource drain), inducing misrouting to weaker/less-robust components to degrade reliability, and exploiting cross-stage inconsistencies to increase jailbreak/policy-violation rates by steering inputs toward weaker, more easily jailbroken components.
limitations: The paper acknowledges: (1) it does not exhaustively enumerate all possible adversary-access configurations for the multi-component cascade threat model, instead focusing on two primary (white-box, black-box) scenarios as representative of common real-world deployments. (2) There is no prior directly comparable attack method for this exact setting (the authors state this is the first study of cascade-system vulnerability), so 'baselines' are restricted to Random-Noise and single-target ablations (SLM-only, DM-only) rather than independent competing methods from other papers. (3) The default attack instantiation is tied to a suffix-based perturbation region and the GCG optimizer (though the authors claim the framework generalizes to other optimizers, tested in an appendix not fully captured here). (4) Defenses tested are limited to randomized smoothing and simple filtering (PPL, regex); the paper does not evaluate more sophisticated or adaptive defenses, and reports that even these simple defenses only partially reduce (not eliminate) attack effectiveness, implying an open defense problem. (5) The analytical vulnerability-gap proof relies on an assumption (final-stage model at least as accurate as earlier stages on routed subsets, with strict improvement on at least one subset) that is described as 'mild' but is nonetheless a condition, not a universal guarantee.

quantitative_results: Joint attack (p=0) reduces cascade prediction accuracy by up to 84.6%
Joint attack (p=1) inflates normalized token cost by up to 148.9%
Joint attack (p=1) increases execution time by up to 108.9%
Jailbreak success rate increased by up to 79-81.4% vs. attacking a standalone strong LLM
Table 1 cascade(1) (open-source, 2-layer): cascade accuracy 78.0%->21.0% (delta -73.1%), cascade cost 10.37->9.30 (delta -10.3%), decision-module accuracy 86.5%->18.0% (delta -79.2%)
Table 1 cascade(8): cascade accuracy 85.0%->52.5% (delta -41.0%), cascade cost 41.2->18.4 (delta -55.3%)
Table 2 cascade(21) (API-based, USD cost): accuracy 93.5%->76.0% (delta -18.7%), cost $0.52->$0.44 (delta -15.4%)
Table 3 randomized smoothing (sigma=0.35), Headline: initial accuracy 78.00% -> Attack p=0 23.00% -> After Random Smooth 29.00% (attack remains largely effective post-defense)
Table 4 PPL filter, Headline: Attack p=0 accuracy 23.00% recovers only to 42.00% after filtering; regex filter recovers only to 50.00%
